--- title: "Examples" subtitle: "Every function, demonstrated" --- ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Install graphfast from GitHub if it isn't already available... if (!requireNamespace("graphfast", quietly = TRUE)) { if (!requireNamespace("remotes", quietly = TRUE)) install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("KyleHaynes/graphfast") } ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rcpp (1.0.14 -> 1.1.1-1.1) [CRAN] fastmatch (NA -> 1.1-8 ) [CRAN] data.table (1.15.4 -> 1.18.4 ) [CRAN] There are binary versions available but the source versions are later: binary source needs_compilation Rcpp 1.0.14 1.1.1-1.1 TRUE fastmatch 1.1-6 1.1-8 TRUE data.table 1.17.0 1.18.4 TRUE Binaries will be installed package 'Rcpp' successfully unpacked and MD5 sums checked package 'fastmatch' successfully unpacked and MD5 sums checked package 'data.table' successfully unpacked and MD5 sums checked The downloaded binary packages are in C:\Users\kyleh\AppData\Local\Temp\RtmpO6GU5Y\downloaded_packages * checking for file 'C:\Users\kyleh\AppData\Local\Temp\RtmpO6GU5Y\remotes3d944c792aca\KyleHaynes-graphfast-5b502c7/DESCRIPTION' ... OK * preparing 'graphfast': * checking DESCRIPTION meta-information ... OK * cleaning src * checking for LF line-endings in source and make files and shell scripts * checking for empty or unneeded directories Omitted 'LazyData' from DESCRIPTION * building 'graphfast_0.2.0.tar.gz' ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # ...along with the optional (Suggests) packages these examples use. for (pkg in c("data.table", "visNetwork")) { if (!requireNamespace(pkg, quietly = TRUE)) install.packages(pkg) } ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in contrib.url(repos, "source"): trying to use CRAN without setting a mirror ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} library(graphfast) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in library(graphfast): there is no package called 'graphfast' ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} library(data.table) set.seed(1) ``` ::: This page walks through every exported function with small, self-contained examples. The chunks are live — the output you see is produced when the site is rendered. A graph in `graphfast` is just a **two-column edge list**: a matrix or `data.table` where each row is an edge `from -> to`. We'll reuse this little graph with three components throughout: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} edges <- matrix(c( 1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 4, # component A: {1,2,3,4} 5, 6, # component B: {5,6} 7, 8, 8, 9 # component C: {7,8,9} ), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE) edges ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [,1] [,2] [1,] 1 2 [2,] 2 3 [3,] 3 4 [4,] 5 6 [5,] 7 8 [6,] 8 9 ``` ::: ::: # Connected components ## `find_connected_components()` The workhorse. Returns one component id **per node**, the size of each component, and the total count. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cc <- find_connected_components(edges) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in find_connected_components(edges): could not find function "find_connected_components" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cc$n_components ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'cc' not found ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cc$component_sizes ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'cc' not found ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cc$components # component id for node 1, 2, 3, ... ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'cc' not found ``` ::: ::: Node 5 and node 6 share component 2; node 1 is in component 1: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} cc$components[c(1, 5, 6)] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'cc' not found ``` ::: ::: `compress = FALSE` keeps the raw Union–Find root labels instead of tidy consecutive ids (the counts are still correct): ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} raw <- find_connected_components(edges, compress = FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in find_connected_components(edges, compress = FALSE): could not find function "find_connected_components" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} raw$n_components ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in raw$n_components: object of type 'closure' is not subsettable ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} head(raw$components) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in raw$components: object of type 'closure' is not subsettable ``` ::: ::: You can pass `n_nodes` explicitly to skip the scan for the maximum id (handy when you already know it, or to include isolated nodes with no edges): ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} find_connected_components(edges, n_nodes = 12)$n_components # nodes 10,11,12 are singletons ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in find_connected_components(edges, n_nodes = 12): could not find function "find_connected_components" ``` ::: ::: ## `find_connected_components_safe()` When node ids are **sparse or large**, a dense `parent` array up to the maximum id would waste memory. This version remaps ids to `1..(unique nodes)` with `fastmatch`, runs the algorithm, then maps the answer back. The returned `components` vector is **named by the original ids**. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sparse_edges <- matrix(c( 1000, 2000, 2000, 3000, 9000, 9001 ), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE) res <- find_connected_components_safe(sparse_edges, verbose = FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in find_connected_components_safe(sparse_edges, verbose = FALSE): could not find function "find_connected_components_safe" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} res$n_components ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'res' not found ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} res$components # names are the original node ids ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'res' not found ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} res$memory_info$memory_saved_gb ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'res' not found ``` ::: ::: ## `find_connected_components_large()` Handles ids **beyond the 32-bit integer limit** (≈ 2.1e9) by keeping them as doubles until after remapping. Set `verbose = TRUE` to see what it decided. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} big_edges <- matrix(c( 1e10, 2e10, 2e10, 3e10, 7e10, 8e10 ), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE) big <- find_connected_components_large(big_edges, verbose = FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in find_connected_components_large(big_edges, verbose = FALSE): could not find function "find_connected_components_large" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} big$n_components ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'big' not found ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} big$components ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'big' not found ``` ::: ::: # Per-edge components Often you don't want a node-indexed vector — you want a component id attached to each **edge / row** of your data. ## `get_edge_components()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ec <- get_edge_components(edges) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in get_edge_components(edges): could not find function "get_edge_components" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} ec$from_components # component of each edge's 'from' node ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'ec' not found ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} ec$to_components # ... and its 'to' node (always equal for a real edge) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'ec' not found ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} ec$n_components ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'ec' not found ``` ::: ::: `return_type = "combined"` returns just the `from_components` vector (same length as the number of edges): ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} get_edge_components(edges, return_type = "combined") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in get_edge_components(edges, return_type = "combined"): could not find function "get_edge_components" ``` ::: ::: ## `group_edges()` and `edge_components()` `group_edges()` is the convenience alias returning one component id per edge row. `edge_components()` does the same but takes a `data.table` and column names, so it drops straight into a `data.table` expression. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Matrix in -> vector out group_edges(edges) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in group_edges(edges): could not find function "group_edges" ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt <- data.table(from = c(1, 2, 5), to = c(2, 3, 6)) dt[, component := edge_components(.SD, "from", "to")] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in edge_components(.SD, "from", "to"): could not find function "edge_components" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} dt ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| from| to| |----:|--:| | 1| 2| | 2| 3| | 5| 6|
::: ::: ## `add_component_column()` Adds a component column to a `data.table` **by reference** (no copy), mapping node ids with `fastmatch` internally. Works with any column names. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt2 <- data.table(source = c(1, 2, 5, 7), target = c(2, 3, 6, 8), weight = runif(4)) add_component_column(dt2, from_col = "source", to_col = "target") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in add_component_column(dt2, from_col = "source", to_col = "target"): could not find function "add_component_column" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} dt2 ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| source| target| weight| |------:|------:|---------:| | 1| 2| 0.2655087| | 2| 3| 0.3721239| | 5| 6| 0.5728534| | 7| 8| 0.9082078|
::: ::: Use `in_place = FALSE` to leave the original untouched and get a copy back, and `verbose = TRUE` for per-step timing on large data: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} out <- add_component_column(dt2, from_col = "source", to_col = "target", component_col = "grp", in_place = FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in add_component_column(dt2, from_col = "source", to_col = "target", : could not find function "add_component_column" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} out ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'out' not found ``` ::: ::: # Connectivity and distance ## `are_connected()` Batched "are these two nodes in the same component?" queries. Builds Union–Find once, then answers each pair in near-constant time. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} queries <- matrix(c( 1, 4, # same component A 1, 5, # A vs B 7, 9 # same component C ), ncol = 2, byrow = TRUE) are_connected(edges, queries) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in are_connected(edges, queries): could not find function "are_connected" ``` ::: ::: ## `shortest_paths()` Hop-distance (unweighted BFS) for each query pair. `-1` means no path exists. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} shortest_paths(edges, queries) # 1->4 is 3 hops; 1->5 unreachable; 7->9 is 2 hops ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in shortest_paths(edges, queries): could not find function "shortest_paths" ``` ::: ::: `max_distance` stops the search early — anything farther returns `-1`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} shortest_paths(edges, matrix(c(1, 4), ncol = 2), max_distance = 2) # 1->4 needs 3 hops ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in shortest_paths(edges, matrix(c(1, 4), ncol = 2), max_distance = 2): could not find function "shortest_paths" ``` ::: ::: ## `graph_statistics()` Degree summary and density in a single pass — no adjacency matrix. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} stats <- graph_statistics(edges) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in graph_statistics(edges): could not find function "graph_statistics" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} stats$n_nodes ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'stats' not found ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} stats$n_edges ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'stats' not found ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} stats$density ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'stats' not found ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} stats$degree_stats ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'stats' not found ``` ::: ::: # Entity resolution / grouping These solve "group rows that share a value across any of these columns" as a connected-components problem. ## `group_id()` The general engine. Give it a `data.frame`/`data.table` and the columns to match on (regex by default), plus values that should never glue rows together (`incomparables`). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} customers <- data.frame( id = 1:6, phone1 = c("123-456-7890", "987-654-3210", "123-456-7890", "", "555-0123", ""), phone2 = c("", "987-654-3210", "555-1234", "123-456-7890","", "555-0123"), email = c("a@x.com", "b@x.com", "c@x.com", "a@x.com", "d@x.com", "d@x.com"), stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) # cols = "phone" matches phone1 + phone2 by regex; "" is incomparable group_id(customers, cols = "phone", incomparables = c("")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in group_id(customers, cols = "phone", incomparables = c("")): could not find function "group_id" ``` ::: ::: Match across all three columns by passing exact names (`use_regex = FALSE`): ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} group_id(customers, cols = c("phone1", "phone2", "email"), use_regex = FALSE, incomparables = c("")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in group_id(customers, cols = c("phone1", "phone2", "email"), use_regex = FALSE, : could not find function "group_id" ``` ::: ::: `return_details = TRUE` returns a rich object with a `print` method showing which shared values created which groups: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} res <- group_id(customers, cols = c("phone1", "phone2", "email"), use_regex = FALSE, incomparables = c(""), return_details = TRUE) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in group_id(customers, cols = c("phone1", "phone2", "email"), use_regex = FALSE, : could not find function "group_id" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} res ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'res' not found ``` ::: ::: Other useful arguments: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Only keep groups of at least 2 (singletons get id 0) group_id(customers, cols = "phone", incomparables = c(""), min_group_size = 2) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in group_id(customers, cols = "phone", incomparables = c(""), min_group_size = 2): could not find function "group_id" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Case-insensitive matching group_id(data.frame(a = c("ABC", "abc", "xyz")), case_sensitive = FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in group_id(data.frame(a = c("ABC", "abc", "xyz")), case_sensitive = FALSE): could not find function "group_id" ``` ::: ::: ## `add_group_ids()` The `data.table` wrapper for `group_id()` — adds the group column by reference. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt_cust <- as.data.table(customers) add_group_ids(dt_cust, cols = c("phone1", "phone2", "email"), group_col = "entity_id", use_regex = FALSE, incomparables = c("")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in add_group_ids(dt_cust, cols = c("phone1", "phone2", "email"), : could not find function "add_group_ids" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} dt_cust ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| id|phone1 |phone2 |email | |--:|:------------|:------------|:-------| | 1|123-456-7890 | |a@x.com | | 2|987-654-3210 |987-654-3210 |b@x.com | | 3|123-456-7890 |555-1234 |c@x.com | | 4| |123-456-7890 |a@x.com | | 5|555-0123 | |d@x.com | | 6| |555-0123 |d@x.com |
::: ::: ## `set_group_id()` An alternative grouping engine using the **bipartite edge reduction** (melt → edges → components). It requires an `id` column and writes the group id back by reference. ### Basic grouping Two rows land in the same group when they share **any** value across the chosen columns. Here rows 1 and 3 share phone `"123"`, and rows 1 and 4 share phone `"111"`, so all three collapse into one group. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt_sg <- data.table( id = 1:5, phone1 = c("123", "456", "123", "789", "456"), phone2 = c("111", "222", "333", "111", "222") ) set_group_id(dt_sg, cols = "phone", var_output_name = "gid") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in set_group_id(dt_sg, cols = "phone", var_output_name = "gid"): could not find function "set_group_id" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} dt_sg ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| id|phone1 |phone2 | |--:|:------|:------| | 1|123 |111 | | 2|456 |222 | | 3|123 |333 | | 4|789 |111 | | 5|456 |222 |
::: ::: ### Choosing columns with a regex `cols` is a regular expression matched against the column names, so one pattern can pull in several value columns at once. Here `"phone|email"` grabs all three. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt_re <- data.table( id = 1:5, phone1 = c("123-456-7890", "987-654-3210", "123-456-7890", "", "555-0123"), phone2 = c("", "987-654-3210", "555-1234", "123-456-7890", ""), email = c("john@email.com", "jane@email.com", "bob@email.com", "john@email.com", "alice@email.com") ) set_group_id(dt_re, cols = "phone|email") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in set_group_id(dt_re, cols = "phone|email"): could not find function "set_group_id" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} dt_re ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| id|phone1 |phone2 |email | |--:|:------------|:------------|:---------------| | 1|123-456-7890 | |john@email.com | | 2|987-654-3210 |987-654-3210 |jane@email.com | | 3|123-456-7890 |555-1234 |bob@email.com | | 4| |123-456-7890 |john@email.com | | 5|555-0123 | |alice@email.com |
::: ::: ### Naming the output column `var_output_name` controls the column written back (default `"gid"`). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt_name <- copy(dt_re)[, gid := NULL] set_group_id(dt_name, cols = "phone", var_output_name = "household") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in set_group_id(dt_name, cols = "phone", var_output_name = "household"): could not find function "set_group_id" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} dt_name ``` ::: ### Values that should never link rows `incomparables` lists values that are ignored when forming groups — empty strings and `NA` by default, so blank cells never tie records together. Add your own sentinels (e.g. `"Unknown"`) as needed. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt_inc <- data.table( id = 1:4, phone = c("Unknown", "Unknown", "555-1234", "555-1234"), alt = c("", "", "", "555-9999") ) # Without excluding "Unknown", rows 1 and 2 would be linked by it. set_group_id(dt_inc, cols = "phone|alt", var_output_name = "gid", incomparables = c(NA, "", "Unknown")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in set_group_id(dt_inc, cols = "phone|alt", var_output_name = "gid", : could not find function "set_group_id" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} dt_inc ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| id|phone |alt | |--:|:--------|:--------| | 1|Unknown | | | 2|Unknown | | | 3|555-1234 | | | 4|555-1234 |555-9999 |
::: ::: ### Treating column sets in isolation By default every selected column shares one value namespace, so a phone number appearing in an email column would link the two. Pass `isolate` — a list of column sets — to keep each set's values separate: identical text only links rows **within the same set**. The columns to group on come from the union of the sets, and `cols` is ignored. Note row 5: its phone number `"123-456-7890"` sits in the `email` column. Because `email` is isolated from the phone columns, that value does **not** pull row 5 into the phone group. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt_iso <- data.table( id = 1:5, phone1 = c("123-456-7890", "987-654-3210", "123-456-7890", "", "555-0123"), phone2 = c("", "987-654-3210", "555-1234", "123-456-7890", ""), email = c("john@email.com", "jane@email.com", "bob@email.com", "john@email.com", "123-456-7890") ) set_group_id(dt_iso, isolate = list(c("phone1", "phone2"), "email")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in set_group_id(dt_iso, isolate = list(c("phone1", "phone2"), "email")): could not find function "set_group_id" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} dt_iso ``` ::: {.cell-output-display}
| id|phone1 |phone2 |email | |--:|:------------|:------------|:--------------| | 1|123-456-7890 | |john@email.com | | 2|987-654-3210 |987-654-3210 |jane@email.com | | 3|123-456-7890 |555-1234 |bob@email.com | | 4| |123-456-7890 |john@email.com | | 5|555-0123 | |123-456-7890 |
::: ::: For comparison, the same data **without** isolation lets the stray phone number in `email` link row 5 into the phone group: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt_noiso <- copy(dt_iso)[, gid := NULL] set_group_id(dt_noiso, cols = "phone|email") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in set_group_id(dt_noiso, cols = "phone|email"): could not find function "set_group_id" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} dt_noiso ``` ::: ### Returning the edge list of pair connections Pass `return_edges = TRUE` to get back the **record-to-record edge list** — every pair of `id`s that share a value, with the shared `value` itself. The group-id column is still written to `dt` by reference, so you get both the grouping *and* the underlying connections in one call. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} dt_edges <- copy(dt_iso)[, gid := NULL] edges <- set_group_id(dt_edges, isolate = list(c("phone1", "phone2"), "email"), return_edges = TRUE) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in set_group_id(dt_edges, isolate = list(c("phone1", "phone2"), : could not find function "set_group_id" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} edges # from / to / value ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [,1] [,2] [1,] 1 2 [2,] 2 3 [3,] 3 4 [4,] 5 6 [5,] 7 8 [6,] 8 9 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} dt_edges # gid still added by reference ``` ::: ### Plotting the linkage with `visNetwork` The edge list drops straight into [`visNetwork`](https://datastorm-open.github.io/visNetwork/): use the `id`/`gid` columns for the nodes (colouring by group) and the returned edges for the connections. The shared value becomes the edge tooltip. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(visNetwork) nodes <- data.frame( id = dt_edges$id, label = paste0("rec ", dt_edges$id), group = dt_edges$gid ) vis_edges <- data.frame( from = edges$from, to = edges$to, title = edges$value # hover shows the value that links the pair ) visNetwork(nodes, vis_edges, width = "100%", height = "320px") |> visNodes(shape = "dot", size = 22) |> visOptions(highlightNearest = TRUE) |> visLegend() ``` ::: # Putting it together A realistic `data.table` flow: take an edge list, attach components, and inspect the largest blob — all without ever building a graph object. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} g <- data.table( from = sample(1:50, 200, replace = TRUE), to = sample(1:50, 200, replace = TRUE) ) g <- g[from != to] g[, component := edge_components(.SD, "from", "to")] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in edge_components(.SD, "from", "to"): could not find function "edge_components" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Size of each component, largest first sizes <- g[, .N, by = component][order(-N)] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(bysub, parent.frame(), parent.frame()): object 'component' not found ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} head(sizes) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in eval(expr, envir, enclos): object 'sizes' not found ``` ::: ::: See [Benchmarks](benchmarks.qmd) for how these scale to millions and hundreds of millions of edges, and [Reference](reference.qmd) for the full argument lists.
